cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are going deep into a book that has quietly rearranged the way a lot of very busy, very capable people live their lives.
It is called Essentialism: The Disciplined Pursuit of Less, by Greg McKeown.

And if you feel stretched thin right now, if you feel busy but somehow not productive, if you feel like you are sprinting all day and yet the things that actually matter keep sliding to the bottom of the pile, then I want you to stay with me.
Because this book was written for exactly that feeling.

Wherever you are right now, driving, walking, at the gym, I want you to settle in, because over the next stretch of time we are going to slow down together and untangle something that most people never manage to untangle in a lifetime.
Here is the hook.
McKeown opens with a story about a man he calls Sam Elliot, a capable executive in Silicon Valley whose company had just been acquired.
He was so competent that after the merger he was rewarded with more and more responsibility.
And he said yes to all of it.

The more he said yes, the more work poured onto his desk, and the thinner he was spread, until he was working late into the night, doing everything at a mediocre level, satisfying nobody, least of all himself.
He was on the edge of quitting.
Then a mentor gave him a strange piece of advice.
Stop trying to do everything.
Only do what you would do if you were a consultant advising yourself, only the genuinely essential, and when a request comes in that is not truly essential, say no.
Sam was terrified.
He assumed his bosses would be furious.
But something surprising happened.
He started declining the meetings and projects that did not matter.
He stopped attending calls he did not need to attend.
He focused only on the work that would make the highest contribution.
And instead of being punished, his work got sharper, his results got better, and he was eventually recognized as one of the most valuable people in the company.
He got a promotion.
He got his evenings back.
He got his life back.
Less, but better.
That phrase, less but better, is the heartbeat of this entire book.
And I want to be precise about what it does not mean, because people get this wrong.
It does not mean doing less for the sake of doing less.
It does not mean lowering your ambition or coasting.
It means less, so that what remains can be extraordinary.
So let me tell you what an Essentialist actually is, because it is not what most people assume.
Being an Essentialist is not about getting more done in less time.
It is not a productivity hack.
It is not about cramming more into your day.
It is almost the opposite.
It is about doing the right things, and making the wisest possible investment of your time and energy, so that you can make your highest possible contribution toward the things that truly matter.
The Essentialist asks a very different question from the person next to them.
Most people ask, how can I do it all?
The Essentialist asks, what is the one thing I should be doing, and what can I let go of?
Let me paint you the first picture, because this book is full of pictures, and some of its most powerful ideas land as images you can see in your mind.
Imagine all of your energy, your time, your attention, your effort, as a bundle of little arrows inside a circle.
For most of us, most of the time, those arrows point in a hundred different directions.
A push here, a favor there, a project for this person, an errand for that one.
Every arrow has real force behind it.
But because they scatter every which way, they cancel each other out, and the circle barely inches forward.
Now picture taking that exact same energy, not one ounce more, and turning every single arrow to face the same way.
One direction.
One big, unified arrow.
Suddenly the whole circle lunges forward.
That is the entire book in a single image.
The person McKeown calls the Non-essentialist scatters their force across everything.
The Essentialist channels it toward the few things that count.
Same energy, completely different result.

It is the difference between light that spreads thin and diffuse across a wide area, accomplishing very little, and light that is focused down to a single point, a laser, capable of cutting through steel.
Now, you might assume this problem mostly hits people who are struggling or failing.
It is actually the opposite, and this is one of McKeown's sharpest insights.
He calls it the paradox of success, and it unfolds in four phases.
In phase one, you have real clarity about what matters, and that focus makes you successful.
In phase two, your success brings you a flood of new options and opportunities, more invitations, more demands, more people wanting a piece of you.
In phase three, you try to take on all of it, because it all looks good, and your effort gets diffused across a dozen directions.
And in phase four, that very scattering quietly undermines the focus that made you successful in the first place.
Success, in other words, can become the seed of failure.
The clearer you are, the more the world pulls at you, until you are no longer clear at all.
So if you are successful and yet feel more scattered than ever, understand that this is not a personal failing.
It is the predictable trap that success itself sets for you.
I want you to really understand the difference between these two ways of being, because it goes all the way down to the roots.
The Non-essentialist thinks almost everything is essential.
The Essentialist thinks almost everything is nonessential.
The Non-essentialist reacts to whatever is most pressing, whoever is loudest, whatever email just landed in the inbox.
The Essentialist pauses to discern what actually matters.
The Non-essentialist says yes to people almost automatically, without really thinking, just to avoid the discomfort of the moment.
The Essentialist says no to almost everything, in order to say a full-hearted yes to the vital few.
The Non-essentialist takes on too much, and their work suffers.
They feel out of control, overwhelmed, worn out, and unsure whether they are even working on the right things.
The Essentialist feels in control.
They get the right things done.
And they experience a strange kind of joy in the journey, because their life feels like it actually belongs to them.
There is a small piece of history McKeown shares that I find quietly powerful.
The word priority came into the English language in the fourteen hundreds, and for the next five hundred years it stayed singular.
It meant the very first thing, the prior thing.
It did not even have a plural form.
It was only in the nineteen hundreds that we started talking about priorities, plural, as if by simply adding an s we could bend reality and have many first things at once.
But we cannot.
When you try to have many priorities, you effectively end up with none, because the whole point of a priority is that it comes before the others.
So let me ask you gently, right now, what is your priority?
Singular.
Hold that question in the back of your mind as we go, because the entire book is, in a sense, a set of tools for answering it and then honoring it.
Now, at the core of this book are three deeply ingrained assumptions.
McKeown says that most of us carry these three beliefs around without ever questioning them, and they quietly keep us trapped in a Non-essentialist life.
To become an Essentialist, you have to replace each one.
Let me walk you through all three carefully, because these are the mental shifts that everything else depends on.
The first assumption is, I have to.
The Essentialist replaces it with, I choose to.
Listen to how you talk to yourself for a single day and you will hear it everywhere.
I have to go to this meeting.
I have to answer this email.
I have to take on this project.
I have to say yes.
But here is the truth McKeown wants to hand you.
Almost none of that is literally true.
You are choosing.
You may not like the alternatives, and the consequences of the choice may be real, but you are still choosing.
When you tell yourself I have to, you give away your power.
You surrender your ability to choose to someone or something outside yourself.
And the moment you give up your right to choose, you invite other people to choose for you.
McKeown makes a subtle and important point here.
We can never actually lose our ability to choose, but we can forget it.
And when we forget that we are choosing, we hand the steering wheel to everyone else.
So the Essentialist reclaims that word.
Not I have to, but I choose to.
This is a small shift in language and an enormous shift in ownership.
Now this connects to something a little darker that McKeown draws on, the research on learned helplessness.
Decades ago, psychologists ran experiments in which animals were placed in conditions they could not escape and could not control.
And after enough of that, something changed in them.
Even when the situation later changed, even when escape became easy and available, they did not try.
They had learned that their actions did not matter, so they stopped acting.
They simply endured it.
They had learned to be helpless.
And here is the uncomfortable question.
Have you learned to be helpless too, in small ways?
A lot of us have.

When you keep telling yourself I have to, when you keep letting other people fill your calendar, when you keep saying yes because you cannot see any other option, you are training yourself into a kind of learned helplessness about your own life.
You forget that you can choose.
The most important thing to remember is that the ability to choose cannot be taken away from you.
It can only be forgotten.
Others can control your options.
They can pile work on your desk and make demands on your time.
But they cannot take away your fundamental power to choose how you respond.
The Essentialist life begins the moment you remember that power and start using it again, deliberately, on purpose.
The second assumption is, it is all important.
The Essentialist replaces it with, only a few things really matter.
This is backed by a principle you have probably heard of, the idea that a small number of causes produce most of the results.
In business it shows up again and again.
A tiny fraction of a company's products often drive the vast majority of its profits.
A small handful of your efforts produce almost all of the value you create.

McKeown points to Warren Buffett, who attributes much of his fortune to being extraordinarily selective, to making a small number of very large, very careful bets rather than spreading his money thin across everything.
There is a line associated with Buffett, that the difference between successful people and very successful people is that very successful people say no to almost everything.
So the Essentialist does not treat all opportunities as equal.
They accept the hard truth that most things contribute almost nothing, and a precious few contribute almost everything.
And their whole job becomes finding those few and pouring themselves into them.
The third assumption is, I can do both, or how do I fit it all in.
The Essentialist replaces it with, I can do anything but not everything, and asks instead, what are the trade-offs, what do I want to go big on?
Let that first line sit with you for a second.
You can do anything you want in this life.
You just cannot do everything you want.
Every yes you give to one thing is a no to something else, whether you admit it or not.

When you refuse to accept that, when you insist you can do it all, you end up letting the choices be made for you by default, by whoever asks first, by whatever is most urgent, by the sheer momentum of your overcommitted life.
The Essentialist accepts the reality of limits, and finds enormous freedom inside that acceptance.
Because once you admit you cannot do everything, you finally get to consciously choose the things you will do.
Which brings us to one of the most important ideas in the whole book.
Trade-offs.
This is the idea that Non-essentialists spend their whole lives trying to dodge, and it is exactly the idea Essentialists lean into.
A trade-off simply means that if you want more of one thing, you will have to accept less of another.
And most people hate this.
They want to believe they can have it all, do it all, be everything to everyone.
So when a trade-off appears, they try to wriggle out of it.
They ask, how can I do both?
The Essentialist asks a far more powerful question.
Which problem do I want?
Let me give you the example McKeown uses, because it is beautiful and it makes the whole idea click.
He talks about the airline industry.
Southwest Airlines built its entire business on deliberate trade-offs.
Herb Kelleher, who ran Southwest, understood that strategy is not about doing everything.
Strategy is about deliberately choosing to be different, which means deliberately choosing what you will not do.
Southwest decided their priority would be low fares, and to honor that, they gave up things other airlines offered.
No meals.
Would you like a meal on this flight?
No, you get peanuts.
No assigned seating.
No first class.
No baggage transfers to other airlines.
They flew point to point instead of routing everyone through big hubs.
They said no to a hundred things, on purpose, because those noes were exactly what made their yes possible.
And it worked spectacularly.

Now contrast that with a competitor, Continental, which looked at Southwest's success and tried to copy it by launching a budget service called Continental Lite, while at the same time keeping its full-service model.
It tried to have it both ways.
It tried to be a discount airline and a premium airline simultaneously.
And it failed badly.
Planes ran late because the full-service side needed connections, budget passengers were furious, and the whole thing bled money.
McKeown calls that straddling, trying to preserve everything while chasing something new.
You cannot.
The trade-off is real whether you admit it or not.
The Essentialist just makes the choice deliberately, out loud, on purpose, rather than by default.
So the next time you feel torn, do not ask how you can do both.
Ask, which problem do I actually want to have?
Which trade-off am I willing to make?
Now here is where the book turns intensely personal, in the story I suspect you will remember longest.
On the day his daughter was born, McKeown faced a choice.
His wife was in the hospital, their newborn baby just hours old, and he had a client meeting on the calendar.
Every sensible instinct told him to stay with his family.
But he felt the pressure to show he was committed, to be the team player, to be the reliable one who shows up.
And so, against his own better judgment, he left his wife and his newborn daughter and went to the meeting.
And the meeting accomplished nothing.
The client was not impressed by his sacrifice.
It did not win the business.
It did not strengthen the relationship.
He had traded a moment he could never get back for something that turned out to be worth precisely nothing.
And the lesson he drew from that day became one of the anchor lines of the entire book.
If you do not prioritize your life, someone else will.
When you do not decide what matters most, the loudest voice, the nearest deadline, the most insistent request will decide for you.
That is what the Non-essentialist life quietly costs you.
Not usually in one dramatic moment, but in a thousand small yeses that add up to a life spent on other people's priorities.
So how do you actually do this?
How do you build an Essentialist life in practice?
McKeown gives you a method, and his favorite way to explain it is a picture almost everyone can relate to instantly.
Cleaning out an overstuffed closet.
Think about why closets get so crammed in the first place.
It is because when you hold up an old shirt and ask, is there any chance I might wear this someday, the answer is almost always yes, and so nothing ever leaves.
Life is exactly like that closet.
It fills up with commitments and clutter that we never deliberately chose.
So how do you actually clean a closet, and by extension, a life?
There are three moves, and everything in the book fits inside them.
First, you explore.
You do not just start yanking things out at random.
You take everything, you lay it out, and you ask a much harder question of each item.
Not, is there any chance I might use this someday, because with that question everything stays.
Instead you ask, do I absolutely love this?
Do I actually wear it often?
Does it look great on me?
You raise the bar.
You force a real decision.
In life, this is the stage where you survey everything competing for your energy and figure out what genuinely deserves it, using a deliberately tough filter.
Second, you eliminate.
You get rid of the things that did not make the cut.
And here is the part people skip.
It is not enough to decide which items to discard.
You have to actually get them out of the house.
You need a bag, you need to know where to drop them off, and you need to actually drive there and do it.
Elimination takes real effort and a little bit of courage.
In your life, this is the part where you say no, where you uncommit, where you let go of the things that are not essential, not just in your head but in reality.
And third, you execute.
You put a system in place so the closet stays clean without a heroic struggle every time.

In life, this is where you build the habits and the buffers and the routines that let you follow through on the essential things almost effortlessly, without having to summon superhuman willpower every single day.
Explore, eliminate, execute.
Hold that shape in your mind, because the rest of what I tell you all lives in one of those three rooms.
Let me start with explore, because there is a genuine surprise here.
Essentialists actually explore more options than Non-essentialists, not fewer.
That sounds backwards.
But think about it.
Because the Essentialist is going to commit to so few things, they are extremely careful and deliberate about which things to commit to.
So they cast a wide net, they consider many possibilities, precisely so they can find the few things worth everything.
The Non-essentialist says yes to the first decent option that comes along, because they say yes to everything anyway.
The Essentialist scans broadly and then selects ruthlessly.
And to select ruthlessly, McKeown gives you one of the most practical tools in the entire book.
He calls it the ninety percent rule.
Here is how it works.

When you are evaluating any option, whether it is a job offer, an invitation, a project, a purchase, anything at all, you score it from zero to one hundred based on the single most important criterion for that decision.
And here is the twist.
If it scores anything lower than ninety, you round it down to zero, and you reject it.
That is it.
No more agonizing over things that score a sixty or a seventy.
A seventy is a no.
Because think about what a seventy really means.
It means you are ambivalent.
It means you are not sure.
And if you find yourself saying maybe, or saying I guess so, or saying it is pretty good, then the honest answer is no.
There is a related version of this that McKeown borrows from the musician Derek Sivers, and it is a line I want you to carry with you everywhere.
If it isn't a clear yes, then it's a clear no.
That mild, agreeable, well, yeah, sure, I guess so, that is the enemy.
Reserve your yeses, your real full-hearted yeses, for the things that score a ninety or higher.
Because when you say yes to the seventies of the world, you fill up your life so completely that when a genuine ninety-five finally comes along, you have no room left for it.
Let me make that concrete.
Imagine you are cleaning out that closet again, and you find a shirt, and you catch yourself thinking, well, it is fine, I might wear it if everything else is dirty.
That hesitation is your answer.
A shirt you are only lukewarm about does not deserve space in your closet.
And an opportunity you are only lukewarm about does not deserve space in your life.
This one rule, applied consistently, will transform your calendar.
It forces you to stop settling for good and to hold out for essential.
Now the explore phase has some pieces I do not want to rush past, because they are about protecting the one asset that makes everything else possible.
And that asset is you.
McKeown makes a point that a lot of driven people find almost offensive at first.
He says you have to protect the asset, and the asset is you.
Your mind, your body, your energy.
In business, if you have a machine that produces all your value, you do not run it into the ground twenty-four hours a day until it breaks.
You maintain it, you care for it, you keep it in top condition.
Well, in your life, the asset is you.
And Non-essentialists burn that asset up.
They sacrifice sleep, they skip rest, they never play, all in the name of getting more done.
And it backfires badly, because a depleted person makes worse decisions and lower-quality contributions.
So the Essentialist protects the asset, and one of the first ways is sleep.
McKeown is direct and a little unfashionable about this.
In a culture that brags about running on four hours, that treats sleep as a sign of weakness and sleeplessness as a badge of honor, the Essentialist does the opposite.
Sleep is not the enemy of productivity.
Sleep is fuel for it.
He points to research on elite performers, and specifically a famous study of violinists, where the very best players did not just practice more deliberately than their good-but-not-great peers.
They also slept more, including naps during the day.
Sleep was not the thing that got in the way of their achievement.
Sleep was what made the achievement possible.
One good hour of sleep can produce many more good, high-quality hours the next day.
When you sleep well, you can actually tell the essential from the trivial, because your mind is clear enough to discern.
When you are exhausted, everything blurs together, and you lose the very ability to prioritize that the whole system depends on.
And then there is play.
McKeown makes a serious case for play, and he means it, not as a nice-to-have but as something essential.
Play, meaning anything you do simply for the joy of doing it, with no goal attached, is not a waste of your time.
It actually broadens the range of options your brain can see.
It sparks creativity.
It reduces stress, which matters, because stress narrows your thinking and pushes you back onto old, worn patterns instead of letting you see new possibilities.
He points out that some of the most innovative organizations in the world build play into their culture deliberately, because they understand it drives exploration and imagination.
And he reminds us that play is how children learn and explore the entire world.
Somewhere along the way, a lot of us decided play was childish, something to be outgrown and left behind.
The Essentialist reclaims it.
So do not feel guilty about the things you do purely for fun.
They are not distractions from your essential work.
They are part of what fuels it.
Now let me move us into the second room, the harder room.
Eliminate.
Because here is the thing.
Exploring and identifying what matters is honestly the easier part.
Almost anyone, if they sit quietly for a few minutes, can sense which things in their life are essential and which are noise.
The hard part, the part that truly separates the Essentialist from everyone else, is actually cutting the nonessential away.
And the single most important skill in that room is the ability to say no.
McKeown is honest that saying no feels awful, and he explains exactly why.
It is hard because we are wired to want to be liked.
It is hard because in the moment, saying yes feels warm and easy, and saying no feels awkward and confrontational.
There is a real social cost, or at least a fear of one.
When someone asks you for something and you say no, you feel the discomfort immediately, right there in the room, on their face.

Whereas the cost of saying yes, the hours you will lose, the resentment you will feel later, the essential thing you will end up neglecting, all of that arrives quietly, invisibly, days or weeks down the road.
So we trade a large future cost to avoid a small present discomfort, over and over and over again.
But the Essentialist learns to reframe the no, and here is the key reframe.
When you say no, you are not rejecting the person.
You are only declining a request.
Those are two completely different things, and Non-essentialists constantly confuse them.
You can honor and respect and even love a person while still saying no to what they are asking.
In fact, McKeown argues that a clear, graceful no earns you more respect over time, not less.
People come to trust you.
They learn that when you say yes, you mean it, because you are not the kind of person who says yes to everything.
Your yes becomes genuinely valuable precisely because you are willing to say no.
He points to Peter Drucker, the great management thinker, who was famously, warmly, and unapologetically protective of his own time, and who was immensely productive because of it.
And McKeown gives you a whole repertoire of graceful ways to say no, because a no does not have to be blunt or rude.
You can pause, and let a little silence sit there, which is more powerful than you think.
You can say, let me check my calendar and get back to you, which buys you the space to make a real decision instead of a reflexive yes.
You can offer a soft no, saying, I am completely flooded right now, can we look at this later.
You can say, I cannot commit to that, but here is what I can do instead.
You can even respond in terms of a trade-off, saying, yes, I can take that on, and here is the essential thing I will have to drop to make room, which would you prefer?
That reframes the whole conversation, because suddenly the other person sees the real cost, and often they will decide it is not worth it after all.
The whole art is to separate the decision from the relationship, so that turning down the request never means rejecting the person.
Now, eliminating is not only about saying no to new things.
It is also about uncommitting from things you have already said yes to, and this is even harder, because of some quirks in human psychology that McKeown walks us through.
The first is sunk-cost bias.
That is the tendency to keep pouring time, money, and energy into something simply because of all the time, money, and energy you have already poured into it.
You bought the tickets to a bad play, so you sit through the whole miserable thing because you already paid.
You stay in a project that is clearly failing because you have invested two years in it.
But those two years are gone either way.
The money is spent either way.
The only real question, going forward, is whether this is the best use of your resources from this moment on.
The Essentialist learns to ignore the sunk cost and ask that forward-looking question honestly.
The second quirk is the endowment effect, and McKeown illustrates it with a study from Duke University.
Students there entered a lottery for tickets to a big basketball game.
The ones who won the tickets, when asked what they would sell them for, priced them many times higher than the students who had lost the lottery were willing to pay to buy them.
Same tickets.
Wildly different value.
And the only difference was that one group already owned them.
That is the endowment effect.
We value things more, simply because they are ours, and we cling to them even when we would never acquire them again today.
McKeown gives you a wonderful test to break this spell.
Instead of asking, how much do I value this opportunity, ask this.
If I did not already have this, if I were not already involved in this, how hard would I work to get it?
If the honest answer is, I would not lift a finger to acquire this, then that is your clear sign to let it go.
You are only holding on because you already have it.
McKeown compares all of this to being the editor of your own life.
And he reminds us that great editing is invisible.
A great film editor makes a movie better by what they cut, not by what they add.
The scenes that end up on the cutting room floor, the ones nobody ever sees, are exactly what make the final film tight and powerful.
Your life works the same way.
The discipline of cutting, of removing the good so the great can shine, is not subtraction from your life.
It is what gives your life its shape and its power.
There is also a tool here I really want you to remember, because it is so practical and so safe.
McKeown talks about the reverse pilot.
In a normal pilot program, you test a new idea in a small way before you roll it out fully.
In a reverse pilot, you quietly test whether removing something will actually have any negative consequences at all.
You stop doing some activity, some report, some recurring meeting, some standing task, and you simply watch to see if anyone even notices.
Very often, nobody does.
That regular report you have been dreading and producing for years, quietly stop sending it, and see if a single person asks for it.
If the world keeps turning, you have just liberated hours of your life with almost no risk.
The reverse pilot is a low-stakes way to eliminate the nonessential, because it gives you real evidence instead of forcing you to guess.
And then there is the discipline of setting boundaries, which is really elimination applied to your relationships.
McKeown argues that boundaries are not a sign that you are difficult or unkind.
They are actually a form of freedom, and even a gift to the people around you.
Without boundaries, other people's problems and priorities flood into your life and take it over.
The interesting thing about a boundary is that if you do not set it yourself, other people will not respect it, because they cannot even see it.
They will simply let their agenda spill all over your day.
So the Essentialist decides in advance where their lines are.
They decide what they will and will not accept.
And then they hold those lines with grace, not with anger.
Boundaries are what let you protect your energy for the people and the work that are genuinely yours to carry.
Now we arrive at the third room, and in my opinion this is where the book truly earns its place, because a lot of writers can tell you to figure out what matters and then say no to the rest.
McKeown goes further.
He tells you how to actually execute on the essential few in a way that feels almost effortless.
Execute.
And the whole philosophy here flips the usual approach on its head.
Most productivity advice tells you to force the important things through with willpower, to push harder, to grit your teeth and power past the obstacles.
McKeown's insight is the exact opposite.
Design your life so that the essential gets done almost effortlessly, so the right thing becomes the path of least resistance instead of a daily fight.
Instead of adding more force, remove the friction.
The first tool for that is the buffer.
McKeown points out that the Non-essentialist assumes the best-case scenario will always happen.
They plan as if everything will go perfectly, as if there will be no traffic, no delays, no surprises.
And so when reality intrudes, as it always does, they are thrown into crisis.
The Essentialist does the opposite.
They build in a buffer, giving themselves extra time and extra margin, because they know things will go wrong.
McKeown tells the story of the nineteen eleven race to the South Pole to illustrate this.
One explorer, Amundsen, prepared obsessively.

He carried extra supplies, he laid down extra food caches and marked them with flags stretched wide so he could not possibly miss them, and he moved at a steady, sustainable pace whether the weather was good or bad.
His rival pushed hard on the good days, under-prepared, left himself no margin, and it cost him everything, including his life.
Preparation and buffers are not glamorous, but they are what let the Essentialist move through the world calmly while everyone around them is firefighting.
In practical terms, this means if a task looks like it will take an hour, block ninety minutes.
If you need to leave at nine, plan to leave at eight forty-five.
Add fifty percent to your time estimates, because we are all chronically, hopelessly optimistic about how long things take.
The second execution idea is one of my favorites in the whole book, and it is about how real progress actually happens.
McKeown talks about minimal viable progress and the power of small wins, the smallest, slowest step.
We tend to fantasize about huge, dramatic leaps, the grand transformation, the overnight success.
But the Essentialist knows that meaningful things are built through small, consistent wins that compound over time.
He points to research showing that of all the things that boost people's motivation and sense of engagement in their work, the single most powerful one is simply making progress in meaningful work.
Not a giant breakthrough.
Just visible, honest progress, even tiny progress.
When you can see yourself moving forward, even by an inch, it fuels your motivation to keep going, and that motivation produces more progress, in a beautiful upward spiral.
So instead of being paralyzed by the size of the goal, the Essentialist asks, what is the smallest possible step I can take right now that moves me toward what is essential?
And then they take that one small step, and then the next.
Do not try to write the book, write one paragraph.
Do not try to get fit overnight, put on your shoes and walk to the corner.
Small wins create momentum, and momentum is what carries you through the long middle where most people quit.
And then comes the power of routine, which McKeown treats as one of the great secret weapons of the Essentialist.
Here is the insight.
Willpower is a limited resource.
You wake up with a certain amount of it, and every decision you make throughout the day drains it.

So if you have to consciously decide, every single day, whether to exercise, whether to do your most important work first, whether to eat well, you will burn through your willpower fast, and eventually you will fold.
The solution is to make the essential things automatic, to turn them into routines.
A routine is essentially a decision you make once, so you do not have to keep making it again and again.
When exercising every morning is just what you do, when writing for the first hour of the day is simply your routine, you no longer spend precious energy deciding, and the routine carries you.
McKeown gives a wonderful example.
When the swimmer Michael Phelps stood on the blocks at the Beijing Olympics, his goggles filled with water mid-race, and he was effectively swimming blind.
And yet he won gold and set a world record.
Why?
Because his coach had drilled the routine so deeply, over years, that Phelps simply counted his strokes and executed on autopilot, blind, without panic.
The routine took over when everything went wrong.
That is the power of a well-designed routine.
It makes the extraordinary happen automatically, even under pressure, because it removes the daily negotiation with yourself.
So design your routines deliberately.
Build cues and triggers into your day that automatically launch your essential behaviors.
Overhaul the routines that are not serving you.
And when a routine goes stale, mix up the sequence and rebuild it around what matters most now.
The goal is a life where doing the right thing is the path of least resistance.
There is one more execution idea, and it ties everything together.
Focus on what is important now.

McKeown talks about the difference between the person who is always mentally living in the future, worrying about what is coming, or living in the past, ruminating about what already happened, versus the person who brings their full attention to the present.
And the lesson is this.
You cannot actually do anything in the future.
You cannot do anything in the past.
The only place you can ever take action is right now.
So the question that cuts through all the overwhelm is beautifully simple.
What is the most important thing I can do right now?
Not today, not this week, right now, in this moment.
When you ask that, the anxiety about the thousand other things quiets down, because you realize you can only ever do one thing at a time anyway.
So you might as well make it the essential thing.
Multitasking, by the way, is largely a myth.
You can do two things at once, sure, like listening to me while you drive.
But you cannot focus on two things at once.
Focus is singular.
And the Essentialist protects their focus for the one thing that matters most in the present moment.
Now let me pull all of this up to the highest level, because there is a bigger story McKeown is telling underneath all the tools and techniques.
It is about living a life by design instead of by default.
This is the ultimate payoff.
If you do not deliberately, consciously decide where your life goes, other people and circumstances will decide for you.
Your life will fill up like that overstuffed closet, with clutter you never chose.
You will drift.
You will be busy, endlessly busy, and yet at the end of it you may look back and wonder where it all went, and why so little of it was about the things you actually cared about.
Remember McKeown's anchor line.
If you do not prioritize your life, someone else will.
The Essentialist refuses to drift.
The Essentialist takes back the pen and writes their own life on purpose.
And McKeown is honest that this is not a one-time decision.
Essentialism is not something you do once and check off a list.
It is a way of being.

It is a discipline you return to, again and again, in every choice, in every meeting invitation, in every request, in every moment where you feel that familiar pull to just say yes and add one more thing.
Each time, you get to pause and ask, is this essential?
Is this a ninety, or is it a zero?
Which problem do I actually want?
What can I let go of, so that I can pour myself fully into the vital few?
So I want to leave you with something you can actually do, starting this week, because ideas that stay ideas are worthless.
Let me give you a set of concrete takeaways to put all of this into motion.
First, this week, find one thing you are currently doing that is not essential, and run a reverse pilot on it.
Just quietly stop doing it and see if anyone notices.
Maybe it is a recurring meeting you dread, a report nobody reads, a commitment you took on out of guilt.
Cut it, and watch what happens.
Most likely, nothing bad will happen, and you will have won back a piece of your life almost for free.
Second, the very next time someone asks something of you, do not answer immediately.
Pause.
Say, let me get back to you.
And then run the ninety percent rule on it.
Rate it honestly, from zero to a hundred, against what matters most to you right now.
If it is not a genuine ninety or above, if you feel yourself hesitating, if the honest answer is maybe, then treat it as a clear no and decline gracefully.
Remember, if it isn't a clear yes, then it's a clear no.
And remember that saying no to the request is not rejecting the person.
Third, protect the asset this week, which means protect yourself.
Pick a realistic bedtime and actually defend it for the next seven nights.
Guard your sleep like it is the source of all your other good decisions, because it genuinely is.
And build in a little play, something you do purely because it brings you joy, with no productive purpose whatsoever.
Notice how it recharges you instead of draining you.
Fourth, identify your one priority.
Singular.
If you could only accomplish one thing in the next month, one thing that would make everything else easier or unnecessary, what would it be?
Write it down.
And then tomorrow morning, before the emails and the noise flood in, ask yourself the essential question.
What is the most important thing I can do right now to move that forward?
And take the smallest slowest step toward it.
Not the whole thing.
Just the next small win.
Then do it again the day after.
Fifth, build one routine around your essential work.
Pick a single essential behavior and attach it to something you already do every day, so it becomes automatic.
Decide once, so you do not have to decide again.
Let the routine carry you on the days when your willpower is low, the way it carried Phelps when he was swimming blind.
And sixth, add a buffer.
This week, whatever you plan, give it fifty percent more time than you think it needs.
Leave earlier.
Block more time.
Stop assuming the best case.
Watch how much calmer your days become when reality has a little room to be messy without breaking you.
Here is the truth at the center of all of this.
You cannot do it all.
You were never meant to.
But you can do what matters, and you can do it beautifully, if you have the courage to let the rest go.
Less, but better.
That is the whole thing.

A life where you have carefully, deliberately, and courageously chosen the vital few, and cleared away everything else, so that you can pour your energy, like a laser, into the things that make your highest contribution and bring you your deepest joy.
When you stop scattering your arrows in a hundred directions and turn them all to face one way, you do not do less with your life.
You do far, far more of what actually matters.
So as you go about your day, whether you are driving right now, or working out, or just moving through the ordinary rush of your life, I want you to carry one question with you.
Out of everything competing for my time and attention, what is truly essential, and what am I finally ready to let go of?
Ask it often.
Answer it honestly.
And little by little, choice by choice, you will find yourself living a life that is unmistakably, powerfully your own.
That is the disciplined pursuit of less.
And it is worth every ounce of the discipline it takes.
Go make your highest contribution.
You have everything you need to begin, right now.
